children.text: The changing racial composition of Columbia Point is corroborated by data from other sources. According to US Census data, 91% of residents in Columbia Point were white in 1960; by 1970, that figure fell to 39%

The Columbia Point projects continued their deterioration after this 1978 report. In 1986, the complex was destroyed, succumbing to the same fate as Pruitt-Igoe in St. Louis and Cabrini Green in Chicago, other massive public housing complexes that were destroyed after they had become dens of crime, drug use and intense poverty. Redevelopment on Columbia Point included new market-rate, lower-rise housing developments, luxury apartments, and the John F. Kennedy Presidential Library.

The same assertion about the practices of the Boston Housing Authority was leveled in the Boston Globe during the 1960s. Writer Richard Hurt identified “a disproportionate assignment of problem families to (Columbia Point’s) apartments. The Boston Housing Authority officially denies a policy of shunting the more unfortunate families into certain projects, but a person in a responsible position in the authority confirmed that such a practice does, in fact, exist.”
For more information on Columbia Point, consult Jane Roessner’s 2017 book, A Decent Place to Live: From Columbia Point to Harbor Point: a Community History.
